coredata.dc:title: Feasibility study of preparation of carbon quantum dots from Pennsylvania anthracite and Kentucky bituminous coals
coredata.dc:creator: Saikia, Monikankana
Hower, James C.
Das, Tonkeswar
Dutta, Trisharani
Saikia, Binoy K.
coredata.dc:description: This short communication reports the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from US anthracite and bituminous coals by oxidation in presence of ultrasonic stimulation. The CQDs formed were examined by using chemical analysis, UV–Visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Fluorescence spectroscopy (FL), High resolution transmission microscopy (HRTEM), and X-ray Photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), revealing the formation of the varied sizes of CQDs ranging from about 2–12 nm. The present method could serve as a simple and a large-scale synthesis of CQDs from abundant and economical coal resources for high-end application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anthracite
Carbon quantum dots
Blue Gem coal
Vitrinite
Synthesis of carbon dots

Basic chemistry and petrology of coals
Observation from electron beam analysis (HRTEM) of CQDs
FTIR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic characterization of CQD
Observation from UV–Vis spectroscopy and FL spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Blue Gem sample CS-1 – A/Collotelinite (ct) and resinite (r). Image CS-1 02 wl. Scale = 50 µm. B/Vitrinite (v), semifusinite (sf), and sporinite (sp). Image CS 03. Scale = 50 µm. C & D/White-light and blue-light images with vitrinite (v), cutinite (cu), and sporinite (sp). Images CS-1 10 wl and CS-1 10 bl. Scale = 50 µm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Anthracite – A/Vitrinite (v), fusinite (f), and sporinite (sp; as a megaspore). Image CS-2 03. Scale = 50 µm. B/Brecciated vitrinite (v). Image CS-2 08. Scale = 50 µm. C/Vitrinite (v), fusinite (f), and semifusinite (sf). Image CS-3 05. Scale = 50 µm. D/Vitrinite (v), macrinite (ma), fusinite (f), and semifusinite (sf). Image 93,950 13. Scale = 50 µm. E/Vitrinite (v), fusinite (f), and macrinite (ma; possibly copromacrinite), Image CS-3 07. Scale = 50 µm.
HRTEM micrographs of CQDs (2–12 nm sizes) at different magnifications (a and b) CS-1 (c and d) CS-2, (e and f) CS-3.

(a) FTIR analysis of CQDs; XPS spectral analysis of C1s spectrum of CQDs samples: (b) for CS1-CQD, (c) for CS2-CQD, (d) for CS3-CQD (e) Solubility nature of as-synthesized CQDs in water under natural sunlight (i) and under UV-light (at 365 nm) shows bright blue fluorescence (ii). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) UV absorption spectra (left) showing the formation of carbon quantum dots (∼260 nm) and (b) FL spectra samples (right) showing the emission around 450 nm (CS1-CQD, CS2-CQD and CS3-CQD).
Proximate and ash analysis of raw coals (wt.%).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: (M: Moisture; VM: Volatile matter; FC: Fixed Carbon; C: Carbon; H: Hydrogen; N: Nitrogen; S: Sulfur; O: Oxygen).

head.ce:title: Feasibility study of preparation of carbon quantum dots from Pennsylvania anthracite and Kentucky bituminous coals

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Petroleum and Coal Chemistry Group, CSIR-North East Institute of Science & Technology, Jorhat 785006, Assam, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Polymer Petroleum and Coal Chemistry Group
CSIR-North East Institute of Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Kentucky, Center for Applied Energy Research, 2540 Research Park Drive, Lexington, KY 40511, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This short communication reports the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from US anthracite and bituminous coals by oxidation in presence of ultrasonic stimulation. The CQDs formed were examined by using chemical analysis, UV–Visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Fluorescence spectroscopy (FL), High resolution transmission microscopy (HRTEM), and X-ray Photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), revealing the formation of the varied sizes of CQDs ranging from about 2–12 nm. The present method could serve as a simple and a large-scale synthesis of CQDs from abundant and economical coal resources for high-end application.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of carbon nanomaterials with some fascinating properties and are considered to be among the next-generation green nanomaterials. Carbon quantum dots, in general, consist of carbon nanoparticles of less than 10 nm in size. They were first discovered by Xu et al. [1] in 2004, during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes through preparative electrophoresis. In 2006, Sun et al. [2] synthesised fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with diameter less than 10 nm via laser ablation of graphite powder and cement. CQDs have been synthesized from carbon-based materials by adopting top-down approaches like arc-discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, chemical oxidation, and ultrasonic synthesis [1,3–5]. CQDs can also be obtained from molecular precursors like saccharide, citric acid, and amino acids by “bottom-up” polymerization [6]. Li et al. [7] synthesized fluorescent carbon nanodots with ultrasonic method and, based on the above results, Ma et al. [8] designed an ultrasound reaction in ammonia solution. Even though there are different synthetic methods for the preparation of CQDs, there is still a demand for easy and low cost approach. Among the carbon sources available, coal is a low-cost and naturally-occurring abundant material compared to other derivative source materials, such as hydrocarbons [9]. Coal plays a promising role as it is a good carbon source for the production of high-value CQDs. It may be preferable to synthesize CQDs from various types of coal as the carbon within the coal structure is easier to oxidatively displace than pure sp2-carbon structures (graphite).

Pennsylvania anthracites and the Blue Gem coal from Kentucky are notable as being high quality coals, albeit for different reasons, thus worth investigating in terms of new utilization technologies. Pennsylvanian-age anthracite has been mined in eastern Pennsylvania for much of the past 250 years; the peak production of about 100 Mt/a being during the World War I years. Coal rank in the Anthracite Fields ranges from low volatile bituminous to meta-anthracite with the mining exclusively in the semi-anthracites, anthracites, and meta-anthracites [10,11]. Owing to their rank, the anthracites have high-carbon content, generally with low-S content, making them attractive prospects for specialized uses such as carbon for direct injection in blast furnaces [12]. Pusz et al. [13] and Nyathi et al. [14], among others, have demonstrated differences in the graphitization of semi-anthracites versus high-reflectance anthracites. Aspects of the coal chemistry have also been reported by others [15-17].

The Middle Pennsylvanian Blue Gem coal, mined in Southeastern Kentucky, is a prime coal used in the manufacture of anode coke for metal processing [18,19]. The high volatile A bituminous, high-vitrinite-content coal [20] is notable in having low-S and low-ash contents, in places, less than 1% ash yield in the whole seam, with an ash chemistry dominated by Fe2O3 with lower SiO2 and Al2O3 than typical for Pennsylvanian coals in the Appalachians [21]. The chemistry shows a Fe-Ca-Mg transition between density fractions in the 0.4%-ash middle lithotype and associations of Ti-Zr, V-Cr, and Cr-Ni-Cu-Mn-Zr-Pb in density fractions of the basal lithotype [22]. Transmission electron microscopy studies by Hower et al. [23] of samples from the latter study showed that the 1.30–1.31-specific gravity fraction of the basal lithotype contained greenockite (CdS), Ni-Co-Ge and Ag-Cd-Bi phases, metallic Bi, nisnite (Ni3Sn), AgCd, manganosite (MnO), and siderite. The 1.22–1.24 specific gravity fraction of the middle lithotype contains La, Ce, Nd bearing monazite and Cr-Fe, Cu-Fe-S, Fe-Zn-S, and Pb minerals.

To date, researchers have used different techniques like physical, chemical, and hydrothermal methods for the synthesis of CQDs from coals [24–28]. The size-controlled, coal-based CDs were additionally produced by the combined technique including acidic oxidation etching and carbonization [27]. Most of the synthetic processes were tedious and involved the use of harsh chemicals like H2SO4 and HNO3 for oxidising the coal samples [29–32]. By using coal as a precursor, graphene quantum dots [25], graphene oxide, carbon quantum dots, graphite nanocrystals [24], onion-like fullerenes [33], nanodiamonds [34], carbon nanosheet-based supercapacitor electrode materials [35], nanotubes and nanofibers were synthesized. In this context, the alternative aspect of coal utilization for producing high-value products is very much essential. In this short communication, for the first time, we reported the feasibility of formation of CQDs from US coal samples. The present report may lead us for optimization of a new and simple method for production of CQDs from high quality coals.

CS-1, a beneficiated sample of the Blue Gem coal, was collected by Hower in February 2018. CS-2 and CS-3 were provided by the mining company for the Hower and Dai [16] study; both represent beneficiated size fractions of a blend of coal seams. The coal samples were crushed to a 0.211-mm top size by adopting standard methods (ASTM 2010) [36]. H2O2 (30%; Merck) was used as received. Polytetrafluoroethylene membranes (Sartorius, lot number 11806-47-N) and ultrafiltration kit (Merck millipore) were used to purify the products.

12 g of each coal sample was mixed with 200 mL of hydrogen peroxide (30%) in an ice-cold condition. The reaction mixture was then ultra-sonicated (frequency: ∼40 kHz; output power: ∼700 W) in a microprocessor-based bench-type ultrasonic bath (Model: Power Sonic 520) for about 5–6 h at atmospheric pressure and temperature. The resultant slurry was cooled to room temperature followed by the addition of a solution of ammonia until the pH of the slurry became neutral. The neutral slurry was then filtered through a 0.22-µm polytetrafluoroethylene membrane and the filtrate was purified by using ultrafiltration kit attached with 1 kDa membrane. The purified solutions were then concentrated by using a rotary evaporator and collect the solutions for subsequent analysis for the formation of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical characterizations of raw coals and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Proximate analysis and ultimate analysis for some samples, heating value, and forms of sulphur were conducted at the CAER using standard ASTM methods [37–39].

For petrology, the coal was crushed to a top size of 20 mesh (840 µm); encased as epoxy-bound particulate pellets; prepared to a final 0.05-µm alumina polish; and examined with a 50x, oil-immersion, reflected-light objective. Maceral identification followed the nomenclature of ICCP [40,41] and Pickel et al. [42]. Vitrinite maximum reflectance was determined using reflected light at 547 nm and a ca. 10 µm measuring spot. The light was standardized against a series of glass standards. Major oxides were analyzed by X-ray fluorescence following procedures outlined by Hower and Bland [43].

HR-TEM images were taken by using a High Resolution-Transmittance Electron Microscope (HR-TEM; Joel JEM-2100, resolution: 1.9 Å to 1.4 Å, accelerating voltage: 60e200 kV in 50-V steps). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded in transmittance mode with 4 cm−1 spectral resolution using FT-IR spectrophotometer (IR Affinity-1, Shimadzu, Japan) and IR solution software. The XPS spectra were recorded in X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (model: ESCALAB Xi + ). Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) and Fluorescence (FL) spectra were recorded in an UV–visible spectrophotometer (Analytikjena, SPECORD-200, Germany) and a F-2700 FL spectrophotometer (2423–008), respectively.

The quantum yield of the coal-derived carbon dots (CDTs) were calculated by using the following formula:
(1)
Φ = Φ r × I / I r × A r / A × η / η r
wherein, Φ is the relative quantum yield with respect to the reference/standard. In the present study, 0.1 mg/L Quinine Sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution (literature QY of 0.54 at 340 nm) was used as standards. The QYs were calculated by comparing the integrated PL intensity (excited at 340 nm) absorbance value (at 340 nm) of the standard with each of the coal-based carbon dots, based on the above equation. Where, “I” is the measured integrated emission intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the optical density (absorbance). The subscript “r” refers to standard index of the Quinine Sulfate. In order to minimize re-absorption effects, absorbance in the 10 nm fluorescence cuvette was kept under 0.1 at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Basic chemistry and petrology of coals
ce:section.ce:para: The semi-anthracite rank CS-3 (2.36% Rmax) and anthracite rank CS-2 (5.07% Rmax) are basically mixtures of vitrinite and fusinite + semifusinite (Table 2). Liptinite is present, as evidenced by the megaspore in Fig. 2 , but, in general, liptinites are difficult to observe in anthracite owing to the convergence of liptinite and vitrinite reflectances [36]. Consequently, liptinites are undercounted but not absent. Aside from the difficulties in observing liptinites, the maceral assemblages (Fig. 2) in the Pennsylvania anthracites resemble those in the lower-rank Pennsylvanian coals in the Appalachians.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation from electron beam analysis (HRTEM) of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The microstructure/ nanostructure of the synthesized carbon dots were investigated using electron beam analysis (HRTEM). In Fig. 3 a–f (for CS1-CQS (a-b), CS2-CQD (c-d), and CS3-CQD (e and f) samples) the formation of carbon nanostructures with different size ranges were observed. The formation of carbon nanostructures with an average size of 2–12 nm was confirmed from HRTEM micrographs. The HRTEM images clearly revealed that the CQDs are spherical in shape and are uniformly sized particles. They are homogeneous and nano-sized, without aggregation. The shape and size of the synthesized CQDs are found to be in accordance with the results reported earlier [25,34,44]. The TEM-EDS analysis also clearly indicated the presence of 94.50 (atomic %) and 4.50 (atomic %) of carbon and oxygen in the CQDs sample derived from CS3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic characterization of CQD
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic characterization of the CQDs is carried out to determine the surface functional groups and the chemical composition of the CQDs. The FTIR spectrums of the synthesized CQDs are shown in Fig. 4 a. The broad absorption peaks at 3232 and 3150 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibration of OH and the presence of COOH group [25,34]. The bands at 1650 and 1680 cm−1 indicates the presence of CO bonds. These results suggest that CQDs possesses aromatic structure with phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface [45]. The obtained C-dots also exhibit characteristic absorptions of CH vibration at 1400 cm−1. Moreover, the sharp bands at 1100 and 1110 cm−1 further indicate the presence of C-O bonds. Due to the presence of these oxygen-containing functional groups, the synthesized CQDs are found to be water soluble.

The chemical composition of the CQDs is also determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 4b-d). The high resolution C1S XPS de-convoluted spectra of the CS1-CQD shows that the C1S XPS peak splits into two main peaks, which are located at 284.10 eV (CC sp2) and 287.60 eV (OCO) (Fig. 4b). From the CS2-CQD, we find that the C1S XPS peak splits into three main peaks, which are located at 283.80 eV (CC sp2), 285.30 eV (COC), 287.20 eV (OCO) (Fig. 4c), and in CS3-CQD, C1S XPS peak splits into three main peaks, which are located at 283.70 4 eV (C–C sp2), 285.30 eV (CO), 286.80 eV (OCO) (Fig. 4c). Thus, the XPS analysis revealed that the coal-derived CQDs sample contain abundant oxygen-containing functional groups (i.e. carboxyl, carbonyl, and a hydroxyl group) and sp2 aromatic structure units [44]. Due to the presence of this high oxygen-containing functional groups, the as-synthesized CQDs is found to be highly water soluble as shown in Fig. 4e (i), which is advantageous for their various applications. Under UV-light (at 365 nm), the diluted colloidal solution of CQDs is observed to be bright blue fluorescence as shown in Fig. 4e (ii), which is a special feature of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation from UV–Vis spectroscopy and FL spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The photo-physical properties of the CQDs were investigated by using UV–vis absorption spectra and FL spectra in order to understand the presence of conjugated pi-domains and optically active defect centres for practical applications, especially in the field of biomedical imaging. Fig. 5 (a) shows the UV − visible absorption spectra of the filtrate containing CQDs. The bands appearing at around 220–350 nm are due to the excitation of π-electrons (π → π*) of the aromatic π system, while a shoulder at 320 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition of CO bonds or other connected groups [34,46]. In CS3-CQD, a shoulder is observed at ∼240 nm. These observed characteristic peaks are ascribed to n– π* transitions of the carbonyl and π–π* transition of the nitrogen heterocyclic sp2 domain, respectively [35]. In FL study as shown in Fig. 5(b), the emission behaviour of CQDs remained centred at 400 nm (excited at 300 nm) corresponds to blue region for all three samples, where maximum intensity is observed.

All the characterization results observed are quite similar with those reported for carbon nanomaterials [24,25,34]. However, structural differences are observed between the carbon nanomaterials which may be due to the differences in the physico-chemical properties of the raw coal.

However, the detailed emission behaviour of the CQDs samples at different excitation, time-resolved photoelectron spectroscopy, life time, and toxicity behaviour will be warranted in future research plan.

In the present investigation, the approximate quantum yields (%) of the as-synthesized CQDs derived from the anthracites are found to be 0.53 for CS1-CQD, 0.20 for CS2-CQD, and 0.04 for CS3-CQD. The obtained values of quantum yield are found to be comparable with the values of quantum yield (%) of 0.38–1.1 for CQDs synthesized from anthracite as reported by Ye et al. [25].

In summary, we have presented a simple strategy to synthesize CQDs from coal by using ultrasonic-assisted wet-chemical method in the presence of H2O2 . Abundantly available coals can be a used as a good carbon source for the synthesis of Carbon Quantum Dots, as confirmed by analytical approach of characterization consisting of physicochemical analysis, UV–Vis spectroscopy, Fluorescence spectroscopy, FTIR, XPS, and HRTEM techniques. The high-resolution C1S XPS de-convoluted spectra of CQDs sample indicated the presence of CC sp2, OCO, COC, and CO functional groups. From FTIR and XPS analysis, the presence of these high oxygen-containing functional groups was confirmed and the synthesized CQDs were found to be highly water soluble. From the HRTEM images, it is clear that the CQDs formed are average 2–12 nm in size. They are homogeneous and are spherical in shape. It can be considered to be an alternative and feasible method to convert coal into environmentally friendly and high-value CQDs. The fluorescence properties of the produced CQDs are needed to be explored for its specific applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Tom Lowe, Blaschak Coal, and Datta Patil, Alden Resources, for their assistance in obtaining the coal samples. Panpan Xie and Jingjing Liu assisted in the collection of CS-1. Authors express special thanks to the Director of CSIR-NEIST for his keen interest to this collaborative research. The constructive comments received from the reviewers are duly acknowledged.
